coredata.dc:description: Recently, g-C3N4 (CN) loaded N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) have been widely studied as promising metal-free photocatalysts due to their impressive performance in hydrogen production. However, deep understanding of the effect of nitrogen chemical states on photocatalytic activity is still lacked. In this work, NCDs doped with pyrrole nitrogen, graphite/pyrrole nitrogen, and pyrrole/pyridine nitrogen were prepared and hybridized with g-C3N4. The characterizations revealed that, incorporation of pyrrole N-doped CDs into g-C3N4 (CN/NCDs-en) effectively enhanced the visible light absorption, facilitated electron-hole separation, and promoted the participation of photoexcited electrons in H2 evolution reaction. Moreover, theoretical calculation showed that, compared with graphite N and pyridine N, pyrrole N has the most appropriate H adsorption ability, which is conducive to the H2 formation. Under visible light irradiation, the CN/NCDs-en exhibited the best hydrogen evolution of 3028 μmol h−1 g−1. These results shed a light on the design and optimization of N-doped metal-free photocatalysts for H2 evolution reaction.

FTIR spectra (a) and XPS spectra of (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s, (d) O 1s of CDs.
The SEM and TEM images of (a, f) CN/CDs-cit, (b, g) CN/CDs-en, (c, h) CN/NCDs-ur, and (d, i) CN/NCDs-trp, and TEM image of (e) CN.
XRD patterns (a); FT-IR spectra (b); and XPS spectra of survey (c), C 1s (d), N 1s (e), and O 1s (f) of different CN/CDs composites.

The photoelectrical measurement of composite materials: (a) UV–vis DRS spectra, (b) Tauc plots, (c) Mott-Schottky plots, (d) PL spectra, (e) Time-resolved transient fluorescence spectrometry, and (f) EIS Nyquist patterns.

Photocatalytic hydrogen production performance: (a-b) photocatalytic hydrogen production rate curves (λ > 420 nm), (c-d) cyclic experiments of CN/NCDs-en, (e) photocatalytic hydrogen production at different wavelength, (f) wavelength dependent apparent quantum yield (AQY) values and UV–vis DRS of CN/NCDs-en.

Optimized unit cells and calculated partial density of states (PDOS): (a, h) g-C3N4; (b, i) CN/CDs-carbon; (c, j) CN/NCDs-pyrrole N; (d, k) CN/NCDs-graphite N; (e, l) CN/NCDs-pyridine N; (f–g) active sites optimization model of g-C3N4, diagram of CDs containing different types of nitrogen, and (m) proton adsorption free energy at different nitrogen sites.
In-situ TPV measurement of catalysts: (a) evaluation diagram, (b) CN, (c) CN/CDs-cit, (d) CN/NCDs-en, (e) CN/NCDs-ur, and (f) CN/NCDs-trp.
Proposed photocatalytic hydrogen evolution mechanism of CN/NCDs-en.
Schematic diagram of the synthetic route of the four type of CN/CDs composite catalysts.

head.ce:title: Which kind of nitrogen chemical states doped carbon dots loaded by g-C3N4 is the best for photocatalytic hydrogen production

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recently, g-C3N4 (CN) loaded N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) have been widely studied as promising metal-free photocatalysts due to their impressive performance in hydrogen production. However, deep understanding of the effect of nitrogen chemical states on photocatalytic activity is still lacked. In this work, NCDs doped with pyrrole nitrogen, graphite/pyrrole nitrogen, and pyrrole/pyridine nitrogen were prepared and hybridized with g-C3N4. The characterizations revealed that, incorporation of pyrrole N-doped CDs into g-C3N4 (CN/NCDs-en) effectively enhanced the visible light absorption, facilitated electron-hole separation, and promoted the participation of photoexcited electrons in H2 evolution reaction. Moreover, theoretical calculation showed that, compared with graphite N and pyridine N, pyrrole N has the most appropriate H adsorption ability, which is conducive to the H2 formation. Under visible light irradiation, the CN/NCDs-en exhibited the best hydrogen evolution of 3028 μmol h−1 g−1. These results shed a light on the design and optimization of N-doped metal-free photocatalysts for H2 evolution reaction.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid development of economy and society, challenges related to energy and environment have become the intractable issues at a global level [1]. As a promising solution, photocatalysis is one of the most attractive energy conversional processes to generate clean fuels from solar energy [2,3]. Since Wang et al. reported firstly on photocatalytic decomposition of water by g-C3N4 (CN) under visible light irradiation, people have been committed to studying this new metal-free polymer material [4,5], because it has the advantages of high earth abundance, environmental friendliness, and easy adjustment of electronic structure [6–8]. However, the large energy gap and poor charge mobility of g-C3N4 lead to its limited utilization of light and rapid photocarriers recombination [9,10]. Recent studies have demonstrated that, modifying g-C3N4 with carbon dots (CDs) can significantly broaden the solar absorption and enhance the charge separation of g-C3N4, so as to improve the efficiency of photocatalytic hydrogen production [11,12]. Specifically, Huang et al. prepared a high-throughput one-photo novel 0D/3D heterojunction (CDs/PCN NVs) for hydrogen evolution, and a large number of CDs with high charge carrier mobility greatly enhance the performance of hydrogen production [13].

Carbon dots, a kind of zero-dimensional carbon material, have shown great application potential in the fields of energy or environment catalysis by virtue of their good conductivity, unique optical properties and easy modification [14,15]. CDs can provide a variety of functions in photocatalytic systems, such as electron mediators, electron reservoirs, photosensitizers and spectral converters [16,17]. Due to the different electronegativity of nitrogen and carbon, the introduction of nitrogen into CDs can lead to charge asymmetry and redistribution of electron density, and thus optimize the electrical properties of CDs [18,19]. Recent researches have proved that g-C3N4 decorated with N-doped CDs (NCDs) possess greatly boosted photocatalytic activity for H2 evolution [20–22]. It is well known that the existing forms of nitrogen doping generally include graphite nitrogen, pyrrole nitrogen and pyridine nitrogen [23,24]. Although many exciting results have been achieved in the CN/NCDs systems, there is still a lack of in-depth understanding of which type of nitrogen doping in CDs can most effectively promote the photocatalytic activity. Understanding the types of nitrogen doping and its role in photocatalytic process is a critical need for the design and optimization of high-efficiency N-doped non-metallic photocatalysts.

Here, CDs doped with pyrrole nitrogen, graphite/pyrrole nitrogen, and pyrrole/pyridine nitrogen were prepared with ethylenediamine, urea, and tryptophan as dopants, respectively. CN/NCDs composite photocatalysts were then prepared by in-situ thermal polymerization of urea and NCDs. The photocatalytic hydrogen production experiments showed that the H2 evolution rate of CN/NCDs increased with the increase of pyrrole-N content in CDs. Photoelectrochemical characterizations demonstrated that pyrrole N-doped CDs can promote the separation of photocarriers and the rapid reaction of photogenerated electrons. Theoretical calculations revealed that compared with pyridine-N and graphite-N, pyrrole-N has the most suitable proton adsorption free energy, which is conducive to the H2 formation.

Urea (CH4N2O) and triethanolamine (TEOA) were supplied from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., ltd. (purity of 99.9%). Ethylenediamine (EDA) was acquired from Tianjin Kemeo Chemical Reagent Co., ltd. (purity of 99%). L-tryptophan (Biochemical reagent) was purchased from Shanghai Adamas Reagent Co., ltd. Citric acid (98%) and chloroplatinic acid hexahydrate (H2PtCl6·6H2O, AR, Pt ≥ 37.5%) were provided by Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. The above materials were analytical reagents without further purification during the experiments.

Synthesis of CDs: citric acid (5 mmol) was dissolved in 10 mL deionized water, then hydrothermal treated at 180 °C for 5 h. After rotary evaporation and freeze-drying, carbon dots were obtained and denoted as CDs-cit.

Synthesis of NCDs: citric acid (5 mmol) was dissolved in 10 mL deionized water, followed by adding 335 μL ethylenediamine or 5 mmol urea or 5 mmol L-tryptophan, respectively. After hydrothermal reaction at 180 °C for 5 h, N-doped CDs were obtained by rotary evaporation and freeze-drying, which were named as NCDs-en, NCDs-ur, NCDs-trp, respectively.

Urea (3.0 g) was dissolved in 20 mL deionized water, followed by adding 0.5 mg of prepared CDs-cit or 0.5 mg of N-doped CDs (i.e. NCDs-en, NCDs-ur, and NCDs-trp). The solution was freeze-dried. The obtained solid powder was then calcined in argon at 550 °C for 3 h at a heating ramp of 5 °C min−1. After natural cooling and grinding, different composite catalysts were obtained, which were named as CN/CDs-cit, CN/NCDs-en, CN/NCDs-ur, NCDs-trp, respectively. As a comparative catalyst, pristine g-C3N4 was synthesized via direct calcination of urea without adding any CDs according to the above steps, and denoted as CN.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of catalysts were acquired under Cu Kα radiation by using X-ray diffractometer (X’ Pert Pro) from PANalytical company in Netherlands. The transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were taken on the instrument of Talos F200S from FEI company in Czech Republic. The XPS spectra were collected on XPS system (Axis Supra, Kratos, UK), and the binding energy of C 1s (284.8 eV) was employed as the standard for correction. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were measured on infrared spectrometer (Nexus-470) of Thero Nicolet in America. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were collected on a RF-6000 Fluorescence Spectrophotometer manufactured by Shimadzu, Japan. The transient fluorescence lifetime were acquired on FLS980 Transient fluorescence spectrometer by Edinburgh, UK. The UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectrum was carried on a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Cary 5000, Agilent Technologies, America). The electrochemical spectroscopy was measured on an electrochemical workstation (CHI660E, Shanghai Chenhua, China). The Na2SO4 aqueous solution (0.1 M) was the electrolyte, and FTO glass coated with the catalysts, Pt foil and saturated Ag/AgCl electrode were employed as the working electrode, auxiliary electrode and reference electrode, respectively. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were measured over a range of 0.01–106 Hz. The Mott-Schottky curves were measured via using the Impedance-Potential mode. The in-situ transient photovoltage spectrum (TPV) were conducted at room temperature. Platinum net covered with power samples was the working electrode and platinum wire was the counter electrode. During the testing process, the working electrode was kept in different atmosphere (saturated N2 or O2). The samples were excited by a laser radiation pulse (λ = 355 nm, pulse time = 5 ns) from a third-harmonic Nd: YAG laser.

The photocatalytic activity experiments were measured on the photocatalytic activity evaluation system (CEL-SPH2N-D, China Education Au-light). The light source was a 300 W xenon lamp (PLS-SXE 300) equipped with a filter to remove the light with wavelength less than 420 nm. The catalyst (10 mg) was dispersed by stirring into a mixed solution containing 45 mL of deionized water, 5 mL of triethanolamine, and 1.0 mL of pre-prepared chloroplatinic acid solution. The instrument was equipped with a GC-7920 gas chromatograph for online detection of hydrogen produced by the reaction, and the gas chromatograph used nitrogen as a carrier gas. The apparent quantum yield was measured under irradiation provided by a 300 W xenon lamp with band-pass filters with central wavelength of 420, 450, 475, 500, and 550 nm. The AQY was estimated based on calculation in the literature [25], as follows:
AQY =
number of evolved H
2
molecules
× 2
number of incident photons
× 100

The density functional theory (DFT) computations were carried out by Vienna ab initio simulation package (VASP) using the projector augmented wave (PAW) method [26,27]. The exchange correlation potential was represented by the Perdew-Wang 91 (PW91) functional within the generalized gradient approximation (GGA) [28]. The cutoff energy is set to 400 eV, and the convergence tolerances of energy and force are set to 1.0 × 10−4 eV/atom and 10−2 eV/Å, respectively. The k-point sampling grid is set to 3 × 3 × 1. The vacuum layer is set to 15 Å. Meanwhile, the DFT-D2 method is used to describe van der Waals interaction [29].

The synthetic process of the catalysts was depicted in Scheme 1 . Specifically, four types of CDs were prepared from citric acid, citric acid and ethylenediamine, citric acid and urea, citric acid and L-tryptophan, respectively. Wherein, ethylenediamine, urea and tryptophan were employed as nitrogen sources to generate different types of nitrogen in NCDs. The four types of carbon dots were then mixed with urea and calcined to obtain CN/CDs and CN/NCDs composites.

TEM images (Fig. 1 ) showed that CDs-cit, NCDs-en, NCDs-ur, and NCDs-trp were small spherical particles with average diameters of approximately 2.6 nm, 3.3 nm, 3.6 nm and 3.5 nm, respectively. All the CDs were well dispersed without obvious aggregation. HRTEM images of the CDs-cit showed very clear lattice structure with a crystal plane spacing of 0.19 nm, corresponding to the (1 0 3) crystal plane of graphite carbon (JCPDS. 26-1080). HRTEM images of the NCDs exhibited well-resolved lattice fringes with a crystal plane spacing of 0.21 nm, which is assigned to the (1 0 0) crystal plane of graphite carbon [30], indicating that the CDs have been partially graphitized. In the NCDs-trp, the interplanar spacing of the diagonally downward lattice fringes is 0.33 nm, corresponding to the (0 0 4) crystal plane of graphite carbon (JCPDS. 26-1080).

FT-IR spectra (Fig. 2 a) showed that, CDs-cit had abundant oxygen-containing functional groups. The out-of-plane bending vibration of the OH group was found at 948 cm−1, and the stretching vibration peaks of CO were located at 1137 cm−1 and 1214 cm−1 [31]. The deformation vibration peak of CH was observed at 1402 cm−1, and the CO stretching vibration absorption peak was found at 1724 cm−1 [32]. Furthermore, the OH stretching vibration absorption peaks were located at 3140 cm−1 and 3498 cm−1. For the N-doped CDs, in addition to oxygen-containing functional groups, nitrogen-containing groups were also clearly observed. The FT-IR spectrum of NCDs-en showed the asymmetric stretching vibrations of C-NH-C at 1142 cm−1, bending vibrations of NH at 1554 cm−1, and the vibrational absorption band of CO at 1655 cm−1 [33]. NCDs-ur and NCDs-trp exhibited infrared vibrational absorption peaks similar to NCDs-en, including the asymmetric stretching vibrations of C-NH-C group and vibrations adsorption bond of CO [34]. Besides, the NCDs-trp showed the stretching vibrations of CN at 1410 cm−1, which may correspond to the pyridine-N structure in N-doped CDs [35]. Besides, the NH stretching vibration absorption peak was observed at 3430 cm−1 in three type of NCDs.

Characterization by XPS determined the surface composition and chemical state of elements in the four types of CDs. The C 1s spectrum of CDs-cit was deconvoluted into three peaks (Fig. 2b). The peaks located at 284.8 eV, 286.7 eV, and 289.0 eV were ascribed to CC/CC bonds, C-OH bonds, and CO bonds, respectively [36]. For the NCDs, in addition to the CC/CC bonds at 284.8 eV, the CN bonds were observed at 286.0 eV (NCDs-en), 286.5 eV (NCDs-ur), 286.8 eV (NCDs-trp), and CO/CO bonds were found at 287.7 eV (NCDs-en), 288.5 eV (NCDs-ur), 288.4 eV (NCDs-trp), respectively [37]. In the NCDs, amino groups may form hydrogen bonds (even form amide bonds) with hydroxyl groups (COH or OCOH groups), resulting in the decrease of binding energy of CO/CO [38,39]. The fine spectra of N 1 s (Fig. 2c) showed that, NCDs-en synthesized from citric acid and ethylenediamine contained only pyrrole-N with binding energy of 400.3 eV. NCDs-ur synthesized from citric acid and urea contained pyrrole-N (the content of 67.8%) with binding energy of 400.2 eV and graphite-N (32.2%) with binding energy of 401.7 eV. In addition, NCDs-trp synthesized from citric acid and L-tryptophan contained pyridine-N (36.4%) with binding energy at 398.2 eV and pyrrole-N (63.6%) with a binding energy at 400.2 eV [40]. No peak was found in the N 1 s spectrum of CDs-cit confirming it did not contain nitrogen. The O 1s fine spectra of the four type of CDs (Fig. 2d) can be divided into two peaks, which were assigned to CO and CO bonds. The binding energy of CO in NCDs decreased compared with CDs-cit, which may be related to the N-containing groups in NCDs. The amino groups of NCDs may form hydrogen bonds with hydroxyl groups (C-OH), which may lead to the shift of the electron of N atom in amino group to O atom in hydroxyl group and the decrease of CO binding energy. The decreasing trend of CO bonds binding energy (CDs-cit 532.9 eV, NCDs-en 532.4 eV, NCDs-ur 532.5 eV, NCDs-trp 532.6 eV) is consistent with the changing trend of N content in NCDs. The changes of CO binding energy in NCDs are possibly due to the reaction of N-containing groups in NCDs precursors and COOH groups of citric acid. The carboxyl group (OCOH) may react with pyrrole, resulting in the change of binding energy. Pyrrole often acts as an electron donor to facilitate the movement of pyrrole-N electrons to the CO bonds, resulting in a decrease in binding energy. The decreasing trend of CO bonds binding energy (CDs-cit 531.7 eV, NCDs-en 531.4 eV, NCDs-ur 531.5 eV, NCDs-trp 531.6 eV) is consistent with pyrrole-N content in NCDs. The structure of CDs (Carbonized polymer dots) is mainly composed of carbon core and surface polymer chain/functional group. In the synthesis of CDs, citric acid containing a large number of hydroxyl (C-OH) and carboxyl (OCOH) groups was employed as carbon source, so CDs-cit contains abundant CO bonds. In the synthesis of NCDs, nitrogen precursors with N-containing groups (i.e. ethylenediamine, urea, tryptophan) consumed some carboxyl (OCOH) or hydroxyl (COH) groups (forming e.g. amides), resulting in the decrease of COH (CO bonds) content. Therefore, the relative content of CO bonds is higher in CDs-cit compared with NCDs. In short, the N-doped CDs with different structures from different nitrogen sources were successfully synthesized. Ethylenediamine, urea, and tryptophan were employed as dopants to generate pyrrole nitrogen, graphite/pyrrole nitrogen, pyrrole/pyridine nitrogen in NCDs, respectively.

The SEM images of all composite catalysts (Fig. 3 a-d) exhibited the appearance of flake like aggregates, which showed that addition of CDs did not affect the basic morphology of the catalyst. TEM images (Fig. 3e) showed that CN had sheet-like morphology similar with the graphene. In the composite catalysts, some small black dots were distributed on the CN sheets. The HRTEM images of the black dots (Fig. 3a, insets) exhibited obvious lattice fringes with crystal planar spacing of 0.19 nm, which is assigned to (1 0 3) crystal plane of graphitized carbon, confirming the successful combination of CDs-cit and CN. In the HRTEM images of CN/NCDs, the black dots showed relatively clear lattice stripes (0.21 nm), which is belonged to (1 0 0) crystal plane of graphite carbon. The high graphitization degree of NCDs endowed the composites high conductivity, which was conducive to the charge transfer.

The crystal structure and chemical functional groups of the composites were characterized by XRD and FT-IR (Fig. 4 a-b). For all the composites, their XRD diffraction peaks appearing at 13.2° and 27.4° matched well with the (1 0 0) and (0 0 2) planes of classical carbon nitride [41]. In addition, all composites displayed similar FT-IR spectra to the spectrum of CN. The sharp peak around 810 cm−1 was assigned to out-of-plane bending vibration of s-triazine ring units [42]. Several intensive peaks at 1200–1640 cm−1 were ascribed to the skeletal stretching vibration of CN heterocycles, wherein the absorption peaks near 1240, 1316, 1410, 1638 cm−1 belonged to CN or CN bonds [43]. The weak broad peaks at 3200–3600 cm−1 were possibly the vibrating of residual amino groups caused by incomplete polymerization of urea during the pyrolysis [44]. All these results confirmed that the CN skeleton structure was still well formed with the introduction of CDs.

The XPS survey spectra of all the samples (Fig. 4c) exhibited the existence of C, N, O species, wherein the composites showed higher oxygen content than pure CN, due to the introduction of the CDs rich in O-functional groups. In the spectrum of C 1s for pure CN (Fig. 4d), the major peak at 288.2 eV was indexed as N-CN bonds, which was the basic s-triazine unit of the CN, whereas the minor peak at 284.8 eV was contributed to CC/CC bonds [45,46]. Besides, a weak peak at 286.3 eV corresponded to the CO/CO bonds [47,48]. In the composite catalysts, the area of CC/CC increased slightly after the addition of the CDs, which indicates that the conjugation degree of composite was increased, or the existence of CDs affected the thermal polymerization of urea. Compared with CN, the peak of NCN bonds in CN/CDs-cit shifted toward lower binding energy by 0.4 eV, indicating that the electrons moved from CDs-cit to CN. In contrast with CN/CDs-cit, the NCN bonds peak in CN/NCDs-en was further shifted by 0.3 eV toward lower binding energy, which was related to the pyrrole-N of NCDs-en. The pyrrole-N acted as an electron donating group to partially transfer electrons from nitrogen to carbon. As for CN/NCDs-ur, the NCN bonds peak was shifted by 0.1 eV toward lower binding energy. The decrease in mobility was consistent with the decrease in pyrrole-N content in NCDs-ur. Differently, the NCN peak of CN/NCDs-trp was shifted by 0.2 eV relative to CN/CDs-cit toward higher binding energy. This may stem from the presence of pyridine-N in NCDs-trp, which acted as an electron withdrawing group to pull electrons from carbon towards nitrogen. In the N 1s spectra for pure CN (Fig. 4e), the major peak at 398.5 eV was indexed as CNC bonds, which was the basic carbon–nitrogen heterocyclic unit of CN, whereas the small peak at 400.4 eV was contributed to the tertiary nitrogen N (C)3 in heptazine units [16]. The peaks of CNC bonds of the four composites shifted to the low binding energy direction by 0.2 eV (CN/CDs-cit), 0.4 eV (CN/NCDs-en), 0.2 eV (CN/NCDs-ur), 0.2 eV (CN/NCDs-trp), respectively. The shifting trend was consistent with the changing trend of the NCN peak, that was, the shift to the lower binding energy direction increased with the increase of the pyrrole-N content and decreased with the decrease of the pyrrole-N content. These shifts demonstrate the strong interactions between CDs (or NCDs) and CN, as well as changes in the surface chemical environment and electronic structure of CN [49]. In the O 1s spectra of pure CN, CO and CO bonds were observed (Fig. 4f), which was possibly caused by the adsorption of CO2 and H2O on the catalyst surface [20,48]. Compared with pure CN (CO 533.3 eV), the binding energy of CO bond (CN/CDs 533.2 eV) in the composites decreased, which is possibly caused by the hydrogen bonds between hydroxyl groups in CDs and amino groups in CN. In CN/CDs-cit, the binding energy of CO (CN 531.9 eV, CN/CDs-cit 532.0 eV) slightly increased, which may be because the carboxyl group of the CDs-cit reacted with the amino group of CN to form the amide bonds, resulting in the electron of CO moving to the amino group. In CN/NCDs composites, the variation trend of binding energy of CO bonds (CN/NCDs-en 531.7 eV, CN/NCDs-ur 531.8 eV, CN/NCDs-trp 531.9 eV) was basically consistent with that of individual NCDs (Fig. 2d), which may be related to the content of pyrrole-N. As mentioned earlier, the carboxyl group (OCOH) may react with pyrrole, and pyrrole acts as an electron donor to facilitate the movement of the electrons of pyrrole-N toward the CO bonds, thereby reducing the CO binding energy. CN/NCDs-en with the highest pyrrole-N content exhibited the lowest CO binding energy.

The light absorbance properties of the catalysts were evaluated by UV–vis DRS spectra shown in Fig. 5 a. After incorporation of the CDs, the light absorption of composite catalysts increased significantly over the entire measured wavelength range, and the absorption edge was red shifted slightly with a visible-light absorption tail-band, which benefited to the full utilization of visible light. The band-gap of CN, CN/CDs-cit, CN/NCDs-en, CN/NCDs-ur and CN/NCDs-trp were calculated according to Tauc-plots method to be 2.52, 2.50, 2.49, 2.50, 2.51 eV, respectively (Fig. 5b). The flat band potentials (EFB) of catalysts were calculated from Mott-Schottky curves to be −0.68 eV (CN), −0.70 eV (CN/CDs-cit), −0.73 eV (CN/NCDs-en), −0.72 eV (CN/NCDs-ur), −0.72 eV (CN/NCDs-ur), respectively (Fig. 5c). All curves of samples show positive slope, which indicates that they are n-type semiconductors. After deducting the reference electrode potential of Ag/AgCl, the CB potential of prepared catalysts was estimated to be −0.89 eV (CN), −0.91 eV (CN/CDs-cit), −0.94 eV (CN/NCDs-en), −0.93 eV (CN/NCDs-ur), and −0.93 eV (CN/NCDs-trp), respectively. Compared with pure CN, the conduction band potentials of the composite materials, were more negative, especially g-C3N4 modified with N-doped CDs, which indicated that they have higher thermodynamic reaction activity in hydrogen evolution reaction.

The recombination rate of the photo-generated electron-holes of as-prepared catalysts were investigated by fluorescence spectroscopy (PL) as shown in Fig. 5d. The PL intensity of all the CN/NCDs composites was weaker than that of CN and CN/CDs-cit. Especially, CN/NCDs-en exhibited the lowest PL peak, indicating the nitrogen, especially pyrrole-N doping was helpful to inhibit the charge recombination of composites. In order to further study the speed of charge recombination process, the time-resolved transient PL decay spectra were measured. Fluorescence lifetime refers to the time required for electron transition from excited state to ground state. For single component materials, the longer the fluorescence lifetime, the greater the possibility of photocarriers separation. For multi-component materials, fluorescence quenching will shorten the fluorescence lifetime, which means the migration of carriers between different components [50]. Compared with pure CN (7.07 ns), the fluorescence lifetime of CN/CDs-cit (5.22 ns) was significantly shortened, which implied that a new transfer channel from CN to CDs-cit was established. When CN was modified by NCDs, the fluorescence lifetime was shortened to around 4 ns, indicating that more carriers were effectively separated. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) analysis was employed to further investigate the charge separation and transfer capability (Fig. 5f). CN has a large arc impedance, indicating its low conductivity. N-doped CDs modified CN showed small radii of arc, indicating that these catalysts have fast electron mobility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic H2 evolution performance of CN/NCDs composites
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic hydrogen production experiments of the catalysts were performed under the light with wavelength more than 420 nm, and triethanolamine was used as a sacrificial agent. It can be discovered from Fig. 6 a and b that, CN showed the lowest hydrogen evolution rate of 560 ± 20 μmol h−1 g−1, owing to their rapid recombination of photo-generated electron-hole pairs and limited absorption capacity. With the incorporation of CDs, the composite materials exhibited significantly improved activity. The photocatalytic hydrogen evolution rate of CN/CDs-cit was increased to 2037 ± 85 μmol h−1 g−1, about 3.6 times that of CN. With the introduction of N-doped CDs, the hydrogen evolution rate of CN/NCDs was further improved to approximately 5 times that of CN. It should be noted that the increasing trend of hydrogen evolution rate, i.e. CN/NCDs-en (3028 ± 78 μmol h−1 g−1) > CN/NCDs-ur (2906 ± 52 μmol h−1 g−1) > CN/NCDs-trp (2748 ± 46 μmol h−1 g−1), was consistent with the increasing trend of pyrrole-N content in NCDs. Among them, CN/NCDs-en modified by only pyrrole N-doped CDs, showed the highest H2 evolution rate of 3028 ± 78 μmol h−1 g−1, in accordance with its unique photochemical properties that it showed the most negative CB potential, lowest PL intensity, and lowest impedance. The stability of CN/NCDs-en was detected by the cyclic experiments under visible light irradiation. In the 4th cycle, the hydrogen production of CN/NCDs-en still reached 2744 μmol h−1 g−1, with the retention rate of hydrogen production of 90.6% (Fig. 6c, d), implying the high chemical stability of CN/NCDs-en composites. To evaluate the apparent quantum yields (AQY), the photocatalytic performance of CN/NCDs-en were measured under different wavelength (λ = 420 nm, 450 nm, 475 nm, 500 nm, 550 nm). As shown in Fig. 6e-f, the highest apparent quantum yield was 3.2% which was achieved at the wavelength of 420 nm. In addition, the trend of wavelength dependent AQY values was highly consistent with the UV–vis spectrum.

In order to understand the different photocatalytic activity of the catalysts, we used the first-principles pseudopotential methods based on density functional theory (DFT) to simulate the electronic density of states of the CN and CN/CDs composites. The CN, CN modified with undoped CDs (CN/CDs-carbon), g-C3N4 modified with pyrrole N-doped CDs (CN/NCDs-pyrrole N), CN modified with graphite N-doped CDs (CN/NCDs-graphite N), and CN modified with pyridine N-doped CDs (CN/NCDs-pyridine N) were selected as the models. Their structures were displayed in Fig. 7 a-e. According to the calculation results, the Fermi level of CN was located at the middle of the band gap without any electron distribution (Fig. 7h), indicating the semiconductor properties of CN. For the CN modified by undoped CDs (CN/CDs-carbon), electron distribution was observed near the Fermi level (Fig. 7i), indicating that the addition of CDs enhanced the conductivity of the composites. It is worth noting that in NCDs modified CN, the strong electron density was distributed near the Fermi level, which implied the high conductivity of CN/NCDs composites (Fig. 7j-l). It can greatly reduce the interface Schottky barrier of composite catalysts during the photocatalytic water splitting process [21]. CN modified by pyrrole N-doped CDs (CDs/NCDs-pyrrole N) showed the strongest electron density near the Fermi level, which may be the reason for the lowest impedance and highest catalytic activity of CDs/NCDs-en.

In addition, the hydrogen evolution activity of the catalyst is closely related to the Gibbs free energy of hydrogen adsorption and desorption on the catalyst surface. The suitable hydrogen adsorption free energy is close to 0 eV [31,51]. This is because low adsorption free energy (strong adsorption of H) slows down the release of Hads to form H2, while high adsorption free energy makes it difficult for protons to be adsorbed [52]. The previous literatures [53–55], have shown that the hydrogen evolution active sites of pure g-C3N4 were the N sites (CNC) at the edge of s-triazine ring units. Hence, we calculated the hydrogen adsorption energy of the N sites (CNC) at the edge of the s-triazine ring units in pure g-C3N4. The calculation results show that the hydrogen adsorption energy of N sites (CNC) at the edge of s-triazine ring is 0.5 eV, indicating that proton adsorption needs to overcome a certain energy barrier. The adsorption Gibbs free energy of H on different configuration of C and N in NCDs (Fig. 7g). As shown in Fig. 7m, the hydrogen adsorption energy of graphitic-C in carbon skeleton of NCDs shows that graphitic-C has great hydrogen adsorption energy (1.6 eV). Compared with N in NCDs, protons unlikely be adsorbed at graphitic-C in carbon skeleton due to large hydrogen adsorption energy. The graphite-N in the CDs had the highest H adsorption free energy of 2.2 eV, which indicated that the adsorption of H needed to overcome a large energy barrier. Whereas, the pyridine-N had the lowest adsorption energy of −1.7 eV, which suggested that it had strong hydrogen adsorption, consequently a large hydrogen evolution reaction overpotential. Noticeably, pyrrole-N exhibited an H adsorption free energy (−1.0 eV) close to 0 eV, which implied that pyrrole-N had suitable proton adsorption and desorption capacity. Photogenerated electrons, which transferred from CN to NCDs, tended to combine with protons at pyrrole-N of NCDs to form hydrogen, and then release H2.

To further confirm the interfacial electron transfer of the catalysts, the in-situ transient photovoltage spectrum (TPV) diagrams of catalysts under different reaction conditions were measured. In general, photoexcited electrons are divided into two parts. One part recombines with the holes immediately after being excited, and the other part participates in redox reaction [56]. The in-situ TPV measurements were performed under N2-saturated and O2-saturated atmosphere, respectively, with a laser radiation pulse as the light source, as shown in the Fig. 8 a. Because of the strong oxidation ability, O2 can be used as an electron capture agent to be reduced to H2O2. The consumption of photoexcited electrons leads to the decrease of photocurrent intensity. By calculating the change of integral area (ΔQ) of in-situ TPV in saturated N2 and saturated O2 atmosphere, the amount of electronic involved in redox reaction can be evaluated. As exhibited in Fig. 8b, the photocurrent intensity of CN in O2 was lower than that in N2 with ΔQ of 0.11. After adding CDs-cit into CN, the difference of integral area increased slightly to 0.18 (Fig. 8c), which indicated that undoped CDs can promote more electron transfer and participate in the reduction reactions. When N-doped CDs were used to modify CN, the difference of photocurrent intensity increased significantly, and the ΔQ were 0.55 (CN/NCDs-en), 0.44 (CN/NCDs-ur), and 0.34 (CN/NCDs-trp), respectively (Fig. 8d–f). Significantly, the change of ΔQ was consistent with the change of pyrrole-N content in NCDs. In particularly, the ΔQ of CN modified by pure pyrrole N-doped CDs, i.e. CN/NCDs-en, changed the most, indicating that it had the most photogenerated electrons to participate in the reduction reaction, which is consistent with its highest photocatalytic hydrogen production activity.

In summary, it can be deduced that N-doped CDs, especially pyrrole N-doped CDs can greatly enhance the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution activity of CN. Due to the narrow energy gap (2.49 eV) and high density of electronic states at Fermi level, pyrrole N-doped CDs effectively improve the utilization of light and conductivity of CN. As described in Fig. 9 , under the excitation of light, the holes generated on the photocatalyst are consumed by triethanolamine, and the photogenerated electrons are transferred from CN to NCDs, which greatly promotes the separation of carriers. The low conduction band potential (−0.94 eV) of CN/NCDs-en with pyrrole-N is conducive for water to be reduced into hydrogen. Moreover, pyrrole-N has appropriate H adsorption and desorption capacity, which facilitates the release of Hads to form H2. These unique properties of pyrrole N-doped CDs greatly boost the utilization of photogenerated electrons, so that CN modified by pyrrole N-doped CDs exhibits the best photocatalytic H2 evolution activity in CN/NCDs composites.

In this work, we prepared N-doped CDs with different nitrogen chemical states, which were pyrrole nitrogen, graphite/pyrrole nitrogen, and pyrrole/pyridine nitrogen, respectively. The NCDs were then loaded into CN through thermal polymerization of urea and NCDs to form CN/NCDs composites. We investigated the effects of nitrogen chemical states of N-doped CDs on the photocatalytic H2 evolution performance of CN/NCDs. The results revealed that, g-C3N4 modified by pyrrole N-doped CDs (CN/NCDs-en) exhibited significantly improved visible light absorption, and electron-hole separation. DFT simulation showed that pyrrole N had a proper H adsorption energy, which facilitated the release of Hads to form H2. With the irradiation of visible light, the CN/NCDs-en exhibited the highest hydrogen evolution rate of 3028 μmol h−1 g−1, which was much higher than that of CN (560 μmol h−1 g−1). This work will encourage new developments in high-performance metal-free photocatalysts and promote their practical application in H2 evolution from water splitting.
